title: Interactive pop-up journal page
subtitle: Adding simple pop-up mechanisms to a journal spread
excerpt: A pop-up in a journal spread creates a three-dimensional element that opens when the page is turned and lies flat when closed. This tutorial covers three beginner pop-up mechanisms suited to journal pages: the box pop-up, the angle fold, and the tab-and-slit mechanism. None requires pre-printed templates.
sourceNotes: Paul Jackson, The Pop-Up Book (1993) documents the engineering principles of pop-up mechanisms. The core angle-fold and box mechanisms described here are widely documented in public-domain paper engineering literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pop-up mechanism formed by cutting two parallel slits into a folded page and folding the tab between them forward. When the page opens, the tab stands up perpendicular to the spread, forming a box shape.
A pop-up mechanism in which a folded strip of paper is glued across the gutter at an angle. When the spread opens, the angled strip forces an attached element to rise diagonally from the page.
The central fold line of a journal spread, where the left and right pages meet. All pop-up mechanisms function by converting the opening movement around the gutter into upward displacement.

content.text: Every pop-up in a journal operates on the same principle: the movement of opening the spread around the
is converted into upward movement of a tab or attached element. The two simplest mechanisms are the
. Both can be made directly in a journal page without templates.

items.name: Journal spread
items.notes: Works best in a journal with a lay-flat binding. Tight bindings resist opening fully and prevent the pop-up from working correctly.

items.name: Craft knife
items.notes: For cutting the slits in the box mechanism. Use a fresh blade.

items.name: Cutting mat, A4
items.notes: Protect the pages below when cutting.

items.name: Metal ruler
items.notes: For guiding the craft knife and bone folder.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For scoring and sharpening fold lines.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For marking slit positions before cutting.

content.text: Close the journal flat. Mark two parallel horizontal slits on the closed page, each 20 mm long and 15 mm apart, centred on the gutter. Cut through both pages together with the craft knife and ruler (place the cutting mat inside the spread). Open the journal: the cut releases two tabs straddling the gutter. Fold each tab forward (toward the viewer) and crease firmly with the bone folder. Close and reopen: the tabs stand up as a box. Draw or attach a small image to the front face of the standing tab.

Cut a strip of card 20 mm wide and 80 mm long. Score a valley fold across the centre. Place the strip across the gutter at 45 degrees, with the scored fold aligned with the gutter. Glue the left half of the strip to the left page and the right half to the right page. Allow to dry with the journal open flat. Close the journal: the strip folds as the pages close. Open: the strip opens and lifts anything attached to its surface. Glue a small element (a photo, a tag, a layered card) to the top face of the angle strip for the pop-up effect.

items.symptom: The box pop-up collapses when the spread is opened
items.cause: The tab folds were not sharpened enough or the slits are too short.
items.fix: Re-crease the tab folds with the bone folder. Lengthen the slits by 5 mm if the tabs resist opening fully.

items.symptom: The angle fold strip tears the page when the journal is closed
items.cause: The strip is too rigid or too wide relative to the gutter distance.
items.fix: Use lighter card for the strip and keep the width under 25 mm. A strip that is too wide creates too much resistance as the pages close.
